content_markdown:
A vacuum flask (or Dewar flask) is an insulated container useful for storing hot or cold liquids (coffee, tea, liquid nitrogen, *etc.*). In its construction a vacuum is used to minimise energy transfer with the environment.

The vacuum reduces

diagram_metadata.description: A cross-section diagram of a double-walled flask with a narrow opening at the top. An arrow points to the space between the inner and outer walls.

vibe_explanation: Conduction and convection both require a medium (matter) to transfer thermal energy. Conduction relies on particle-to-particle contact, and convection relies on the bulk movement of fluids. By removing the air to create a vacuum, these two pathways are effectively eliminated. Radiation, however, consists of electromagnetic waves and can travel through a vacuum, so it is not reduced by the vacuum itself (it is usually reduced by silvering the walls).
